ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for red bean paste with lotus seed sweet soup. FOODON_03311737 (adzuki bean food product) is used as the closest parent. A more specific term for Chinese tong sui dessert soups is absent from FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (sweet bean paste) is the closest defensible match. The specific combination with lotus seed and tangerine peel is not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for red bean paste with lotus seed dessert soup. SNOMED CT does not cover this level of culinary specificity for Chinese dessert soups.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of red bean paste with lotus seed sweet soup, approximately 250 g, as typically served in a Chinese dessert shop (tong sui pou).

nutrient_source.title: Red beans, boiled (adzuki) and lotus seeds, cooked — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish (red bean paste with lotus seed sweet soup). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Adzuki beans, cooked, boiled (FDC ID 173735) and Lotus seeds, dried (FDC ID 170082), combined in approximate proportions typical of this dessert (approximately 55% cooked red bean paste, 15% lotus seeds, 25% sweetened liquid, 5% tangerine peel contribution negligible). Sugar content adjusted upward to reflect added sugar typical of tong sui preparation. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for the finished sweet soup as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Adzuki beans, boiled; Lotus seeds analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 34 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled adzuki beans (GI approximately 34, low category), which is the primary glycemic driver of this dish. Lotus seeds have a low GI consistent with other legume seeds. Added sugar raises the effective GI slightly but the dominant ingredient (adzuki bean) anchors the overall GI in the low range. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (34 × 19.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.5) / 100 = approximately 16.5 g. This is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement of this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content; potassium-rich legume base
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 18 mg sodium per 100 g and a meaningful potassium content (~210 mg/100 g) from adzuki beans and lotus seeds, this dessert soup is suitable for hypertensive individuals when consumed without additional salt and in moderate portion sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar content and moderate glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the glycemic index is low (GI ~34) due to the adzuki bean base, the added sugar (~9 g/100 g) and a glycemic load of approximately 16.5 per standard 250 g bowl place this dessert in the moderate concern category for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control and infrequent consumption are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat; dietary fibre from legumes; plant-based ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content (~0.1 g/100 g), absence of cholesterol, and dietary fibre (~2.8 g/100 g) from adzuki beans and lotus seeds support a heart-healthy profile, making this dessert a relatively favourable choice for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk when consumed in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red Bean Paste with Lotus Seed (红豆莲子糖水, Hóng Dòu Lián Zǐ Tāng Shuǐ) is a traditional Chinese dessert soup (tong sui) widely enjoyed in southern China, Hong Kong, and Singapore. It is prepared by slow-cooking red adzuki beans until they reach a velvety, paste-like consistency, then combining them with plump, tender lotus seeds in a lightly sweetened broth. Aged Xinhui tangerine peel (chenpi) is often added to impart a subtle citrus aroma and a mildly bitter counterpoint to the sweetness. The texture is thick and smooth from the bean paste, punctuated by the soft, slightly starchy bite of the lotus seeds. The flavour profile is gently sweet, earthy, and faintly floral. Nutritionally, this dessert provides moderate carbohydrates (~19.5 g/100 g), modest protein (~3.8 g/100 g) from the legume base, very low fat (~0.4 g/100 g), and useful dietary fibre (~2.8 g/100 g). The glycemic index is low (~34) anchored by the adzuki bean, though added sugar raises the glycemic load to approximately 16.5 per standard 250 g serving. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, it is low in sodium and saturated fat, making it broadly suitable for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients in moderate portions; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size due to added sugar. It is a staple of Chinese dessert shops (糖水铺, tong sui pou) and is commonly consumed as an after-meal dessert or afternoon snack.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked adzuki beans and lotus seeds in proportions typical of this dessert. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled adzuki beans as the primary glycemic driver. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-defined and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (adzuki beans cooked FDC 173735; lotus seeds dried FDC 170082) and adjusted for typical tong sui preparation with added sugar. No HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry was found for this specific dish. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish in the source databases used. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this plant-based dessert. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero by formulation. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; confirmed zero by formulation. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used; confirmed zero by formulation. 9. GI of 34 is an analogous estimate based on boiled adzuki beans from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement of this specific dish was found. 10. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific dish. 11. Serving size of 250 g is an estimate for a standard bowl as served in a Singapore Chinese dessert shop.
